    "reference_id": "10",
    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 34-year-old male, a computer engineer by profession, was found drowsy at home and was referred to a nearby medical center. There was history of a recent business trip to Greece which took almost 6 weeks. He was suffering from headache and fever and was drowsy when admitted in the hospital [modified Rankin scale (MRS) =2]. Preliminary workups were all negative and brain computed tomography scan (CTS) showed a small left frontal opercular intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) and intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH) accompanied with mild hydrocephalus [Figure 1]. He refused to stay in the hospital for further investigations after improvement of his mental state. Headache, fever, and intermittent changes in his state of consciousness recurred after a month and he was admitted in our center after another attack of severe headache. At the time of admission, he was stuporous with mild right hemiparesis and pure motor dysphasia (MRS = 4). Brain CTS showed a left frontal opercular ICH [<PMC4228499_SNI-5-152-g003_undivided_1_1.webp>]. Four-vessel digital subtraction angiography (DSA) showed a left distal middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm that was 12 x 9 mm in diameter and without an identifiable neck [<PMC4228499_SNI-5-152-g004_undivided_1_1.webp>]. This aneurysm was visible in both anteroposterior (AP) and lateral views even though there were two other ill-defined aneurysm blebs only seen in the lateral view of the angiogram. These two blebs were located along the more proximal branches of M3 segment of left MCA [<PMC4228499_SNI-5-152-g005_undivided_1_1.webp>].\\nConsidering the past history of fever, headache, detectable neck rigidity, and ICH, the first impression was rupture of a mycotic aneurysm. None of the aneurysms had well-identifiable neck in the angiogram. It was planned to treat the presumable mycotic lesions with a trial of wide-spectrum antibiotic therapy including Vancomycin 1 g twice/day + Rifampin 600 mg three times/day and Tazocin 4.5 g three times/day. All of the preclinical studies were negative except for the following: erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) =28, C-reactive protein (CRP) =3 plus, and positive serology for brucellosis (direct Coomb's test/Brucella micro agglutination test) both in his plasma and CSF with the agglutinin titer of 1/160 and 1/80, respectively. CSF analysis was compatible with partially treated meningitis: WBC = 500/mm3, polymorphoneuclears 60%, lymphocytes = 40%, protein = 55.3mg/dl, and sugar = 56 mg/dl.\\nHis general condition improved moderately after 3 weeks of antibiotic therapy (MRS 3), but intermittent fever persisted and the CSF picture did not change notably. A brain computed tomographic angiography (CTA) did not show any regression in the size of the distal MCA aneurysm. The other two ill-defined aneurysms noted in the previous DSA were well elucidated in the new CTA [Figure 5].\\nConsidering the available facilities, it was decided to treat the aneurysms surgically and it was planned for either clipping-only or excision with reconstruction bypass surgery of the aneurysms according to the findings during surgery. A left posterior temporoparietal craniotomy was done after 30 days of antibiotic therapy. The dura was opened, taking care of the arachnoidal adhesions visible beneath the dura, and attached to one of the cortically exposed aneurysms. A small aneurysm was located along one of the cortical branches of M4, along the superior temporal gyrus with adhesions in the surrounding subarachnoid space. The arachnoid could be dissected sharply. The aneurysm was a small one located on a distal M4 branch, without a clippable neck. It was coagulated and excised. The second aneurysm was located along M3 segment of the frontal branch of MCA. It was dissected, clipped, and resected. The clip could be removed after micro-coagulation of the parent artery [Figure 6a and b]. The more distal dissection of Sylvian fissure revealed a third small aneurysm located more distally along the M4 segment of MCA, which was dissected, clipped, coagulated, and excised. The histopathological examination of the resected aneurysms was compatible with \\\"mycotic aneurysm\\\" [Figure 7a-c].\\nThe patient's postoperative course was uneventful. Considering the positive serology for brucellosis both in plasma and CSF, the anti-brucella antibiotic regimen was continued. The patient could be discharged after 1 month with improvement of fever and stuttering (MRS 2). The anti-brucella medications were continued for another 6 weeks and negative serology tests achieved. The postoperative CTA performed after 1 year confirmed no aneurysmal dilatation along the course of MCA [Figure 8].\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Brucellosis\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Brucellosis\"\n  }\n]\n\nAttached image descriptions:\n- CT head scan shows a mass in the right frontal lobe.\nThe mass appears to be causing some displacement of the brain.\nThere is edema surrounding the mass.\nThe mass is approximately 3 cm in diameter.\n- *   Right cavernous sinus aneurysm\n*   CT angiogram\n*   Anatomy\n- Cerebral angiogram.\nVisible blood vessels.\nNo obvious lesions.\nNo devices.",
    "file_path": "custom_case_13327.json",
    "chunk_id": "chunk-1c81c9a95031775b8ada47cea0846e36"
  }
]

case_text: A 45-year-old Caucasian male patient presented at our emergency department with headache, visual deficiencies, and moderate disorientation. On the day of the admission, he experienced a convulsive seizure. His medical history was free of any severe infections, immunomodulatory diseases, severe traumas, malignancies, and recent surgical interventions. In the past years, he had not visited any tropical or subtropical areas.\nLaboratory parameters on admission were not deranged and physical examination showed normothermia without meningeal irritation. In addition to the visual deficiencies and the mild mnestic impairment, no neurological deficits could be identified.\nImmediate computed tomography (CT) scan showed a hypodense lesion in the right parietal region. On contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a ring-shaped lesion in the right parietal lobe was noted [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_a_1_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_b_2_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_c_3_4.webp>]

    "entity_name": "brain",
    "entity_type": "anatomical_location",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Brain**\n\nThe brain serves as the central nervous system (CNS) organ, housing multiple critical structures responsible for processing sensory input, regulating motor function, and maintaining homeostasis. While the brain is the primary site for most space-occupying lesions, its involvement is not universally common, particularly in certain clinical contexts. Lesions within the brain may manifest in MRI scans as focal or diffuse abnormalities, often characterized by alterations in tissue density, contrast enhancement, or signal intensity depending on etiology (e.g., neoplastic, inflammatory, or ischemic).\n\nIn cases of space-occupying lesions, the brain’s location within the cranial cavity dictates clinical presentation, including mass effect phenomena such as herniation syndromes, elevated intracranial pressure, and associated systemic sequelae. When sampled for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, the CNS region sampled may include cortical, subcortical, or deep gray matter structures, depending on the lesion’s laterality and depth.\n\nNotably, severe meningioencephalitis—an inflammatory process affecting both the meninges and brain parenchyma—can lead to fatal outcomes, such as the patient’s reported sudden cardiac arrest. This underscores the potential for life-threatening complications when CNS involvement is severe or untreated. While the brain itself is a fundamental anatomical entity, its clinical relevance extends to systemic interactions, particularly in conditions like meningioencephalitis, where multisystem involvement may occur.<SEP>brain",

    "description": "**Abnormal Masses Across Anatomical Domains: Comprehensive Clinical Summary**\n\nThe provided descriptions collectively detail **abnormal growths or lesions** affecting multiple anatomical domains, characterized by variable presentations and imaging findings. Key observations include intracranial, cardiac, abdominal, and musculoskeletal masses, with differential diagnostic considerations spanning neoplastic, inflammatory, and congenital etiologies.\n\n**Central Nervous System (CNS) Masses**\nEvidence suggests focal intracranial lesions, such as a **right frontal lobe abnormality** causing localized mass effect on adjacent structures. Radiological imaging (e.g., MRI axial views) demonstrates a lesion exerting compression, though definitive etiology remains indeterminate. Primary brain tumors (e.g., gliomas), metastatic disease, inflammatory lesions, or congenital anomalies are potential differential diagnoses. Further diagnostic evaluation, including biopsy or contrast-enhanced studies, is essential for precise classification.\n\n**Cardiac Mass**\nA cardiac lesion is noted, potentially impacting cardiac function. While details are limited, such masses may represent benign tumors (e.g., myxomas) or malignant processes (e.g., sarcomas), necessitating further diagnostic workup.\n\n**Abdominal Masses**\nAbdominal imaging reveals a **palpable right upper quadrant mass** with a **3 cm diameter lesion** on CT scan. Additional findings include a **1.5 cm lesion in Couinaud’s segment 8 of the liver**, suggestive of a solid organ lesion (e.g., renal, adrenal, or retroperitoneal tumor). Potential etiologies include neoplastic processes, inflammatory pseudotumors, or reactive hyperplasia. Differential diagnoses include benign lesions, infections, or degenerative changes.\n\n**Lung Masses**\nChest CT scans identify masses in the **right lung (3 cm diameter)** and **left upper lobe**, with potential neoplastic or non-neoplastic causes. Further evaluation, including functional studies, is required to determine etiology.\n\n**Musculoskeletal Masses**\nRepeated descriptions of a **mass in the left lower extremity** adjacent to a hematoma suggest a localized lesion. While imaging details are limited, potential etiologies include neoplastic processes (e.g., soft-tissue sarcomas), inflammatory conditions, or traumatic changes. Differential diagnoses include benign tumors, infections, or degenerative lesions.\n\nCommon clinical features include **mass effect on adjacent structures**, though severity varies. Imaging findings are critical for characterization, but definitive diagnosis requires additional diagnostic modalities (e.g., biopsy, contrast-enhanced studies). Comorbidities influencing presentation or progression are not detailed but may include immunosuppression or metabolic disorders.",

    "entity_type": "clinical_event",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary of Lesions**\n\nA *lesion* denotes an abnormal pathological finding that may manifest across various anatomical domains, including soft tissues, bony structures, and visceral organs. The provided descriptions highlight lesions in diverse contexts, particularly emphasizing intracranial and extracranial sites. In the temporal bone, lesions are identified as structural abnormalities detectable via imaging, though their etiology—whether neoplastic, inflammatory, or degenerative—remains indeterminate. When intracranial, lesions frequently exceed 2 cm in diameter, with laterality variably documented, though bilateral involvement in the parietal lobes is noted in some cases.\n\nCardiovascular-related terminology, such as an *abnormality in the right coronary artery*, is included but does not constitute a traditional lesion. Chronicity is evident, as lesions persist for at least three months, raising concerns for underlying etiologies such as infection, ischemic changes, or neoplastic progression. Recurrence at surgical sites suggests a persistent pathological process, potentially indicating an aggressive or high-risk lesion type.\n\nMRI findings further detail lesions as hyperintense masses or consistent with progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) lesions. Specific localization includes the left parietal lobe, with generalized descriptions indicating bilateral involvement in the brain. Clinical ambiguity persists regarding lesion etiology, staging, or laterality in some cases, particularly when terms like *\"abnormal finding\"* are used without specific diagnostic criteria.   {
    "entity_name": "brain abscess",
    "entity_type": "disease_disorder",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Brain Abscess**\n\nA **brain abscess** represents a focal, localized infectious lesion within the brain parenchyma or adjacent structures, characterized by purulent material accumulation. Imaging studies, including computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), commonly demonstrate a ring-enhancing lesion, often in the right frontal lobe, with medial extension into the frontal horn of the right lateral ventricle. While the right frontal region is frequently affected, abscesses may also occur in other cerebral regions, including the temporo-parietal-occipital junction. The diagnosis is typically confirmed by the presence of thick purulent fluid within the lesion, often linked to systemic or local infectious sources such as dental or sinus infections.\n\nClinical presentations vary based on lesion size, location, and surrounding edema, often manifesting as focal neurological deficits, headache, or altered mental status. Differential diagnoses include metastatic tumors, granulomatous infections, and other ring-enhancing lesions, though the purulent nature and imaging characteristics strongly support the diagnosis of an abscess. Management generally involves a combination of surgical drainage (e.g., stereotactic aspiration or craniotomy) and systemic antimicrobial therapy, tailored to the identified pathogen. Comorbidities such as immunosuppression or dental infections may influence prognosis and treatment planning.\n\nNotably, the provided descriptions indicate variability in suspected locations, including the left cerebellar lobe, though the primary clinical consideration remains consistent with the general clinical presentation and diagnostic criteria outlined above. The presence of *Bacillus cereus* further supports the diagnosis in specific clinical contexts.<SEP>brain abscess described as locoregional extension from infection<SEP>patient's prior diagnosis of brain infection<SEP>brain abscess detected by MRI.",

    "entity_name": "edema",
    "entity_type": "clinical_event",
    "description": "**Edema** is a clinically significant condition characterized by the abnormal accumulation of interstitial fluid due to disturbances in capillary permeability, lymphatic function, or systemic hemodynamic balance. The provided descriptions highlight distinct presentations—ranging from localized inflammatory or infectious etiologies to systemic and neurological manifestations—requiring careful differentiation based on anatomical context, severity, and associated comorbidities.\n\n**Neurological Edema**: Cerebral edema, including findings in the cerebral cortex, temporal, and occipital lobes, often reflects intracranial pathology. Multifocal white matter changes (e.g., demyelinating plaques or toxic/metabolic encephalopathy) suggest underlying demyelinating diseases (e.g., multiple sclerosis) or encephalopathies. Urgent neuroimaging and metabolic screening are essential in suspected cases, given the risk of intracranial hypertension or herniation.\n\n**Spinal Cord Edema**: Histopathological evidence of spinal cord edema may indicate compressive or inflammatory processes, such as transverse myelitis, spinal cord tumors, or traumatic injury. Differential diagnosis includes vascular events (e.g., spinal artery occlusion) or infectious/inflammatory conditions (e.g., discitis).\n\n**Localized Edema**: Observed swelling in the left hand, cervicocranial region, or foot (left foot) often points to inflammatory/infectious causes (e.g., cellulitis, lymphadenitis) or post-surgical sequelae. Absence of edema on physical exam in some cases may mask occult systemic conditions (e.g., hypoalbuminemia, lymphatic obstruction). Transient soft tissue edema post-surgery may exacerbate in patients with diabetes mellitus or venous insufficiency.\n\n**Systemic Edema**: Bilateral lower limb edema, when present, aligns with systemic conditions such as congestive heart failure or nephrotic syndrome. The presence of gangrene and hypotension suggests severe systemic illness, necessitating hemodynamic and infectious disease evaluation. Systemic edema may also arise from hypoalbuminemia or lymphatic dysfunction, requiring renal and hepatic function assessment.\n\n**Clinical Implications**: Edema’s presentation varies widely, from localized inflammation to generalized fluid overload. Differential diagnosis must account for anatomical context, severity, and comorbidities. Neurological edema demands urgent neuroimaging and metabolic workup, while localized edema requires targeted infectious/inflammatory evaluation. Systemic edema necessitates hemodynamic and renal assessment, particularly in patients with hypotension or gangrene. The absence of edema in MRI findings (e.g., no cerebral edema) does not exclude systemic or spinal involvement, underscoring the need for context-specific diagnostic evaluation.<SEP>Histopathology showed edema.<SEP>edema is described in the MRI.<SEP>no edema observed in chest X-ray<SEP>swelling in placental tissue",

    "reference_id": "1",
    "content": "[
  {
    "case_text": "A 45-year-old Caucasian male patient presented at our emergency department with headache, visual deficiencies, and moderate disorientation. On the day of the admission, he experienced a convulsive seizure. His medical history was free of any severe infections, immunomodulatory diseases, severe traumas, malignancies, and recent surgical interventions. In the past years, he had not visited any tropical or subtropical areas.\nLaboratory parameters on admission were not deranged and physical examination showed normothermia without meningeal irritation. In addition to the visual deficiencies and the mild mnestic impairment, no neurological deficits could be identified.\nImmediate computed tomography (CT) scan showed a hypodense lesion in the right parietal region. On contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a ring-shaped lesion in the right parietal lobe was noted [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_a_1_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_b_2_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_c_3_4.webp>]. The diffusion-weighted images showed an intensive hyperintense signal, which was strongly suspicious for a brain abscess [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_d_4_4.webp>].\nCalculated antibiotic treatment was initiated with amoxicillin/clavulanic acid and metronidazole. The patient was prepared for surgical intervention. The patient was informed regarding the off-label use of FL, and he provided written consent.\nApproximately 30-45 minutes prior to skin incision, 5 mg/kg bodyweight of FL (Natrium Fluoreszein  10%, ALCON, Germany) was administered via the central venous line during induction of anesthesia.\nCraniotomy was planned with neuronavigation. After durotomy, the PENTERO 900 surgical microscope was applied. The lesion could not be detected under white light, however, showed intensive fluorescent staining under the YE560 nm filter [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g002_a_1_2.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g002_b_2_2.webp>]. After opening of the capsule, viscous pus poured out [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g003_a_1_2.webp>] that was not fluorescing under filtered light [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g003_b_2_2.webp>]. The pus and the capsule were microsurgically removed and sent to the neuropathology and microbiology department.\nThe postoperative course was uneventful. Because no microbiological specimen could be identified, calculated antibiotic treatment was continued. The visual deficiencies and the mnestic impairment completely resolved, and the patient was discharged after 5 weeks.",
    "extracted_disease_name": "Brain abscess",
    "grouped_disease_name": "Abscess"
  }
]

    "reference_id": "2",
    "content": "[
  {
    "case_text": "A 24-year-old female was brought to the emergency department with complaints of altered mental status and recurrent seizures associated with low-grade fever, headache, and altered sensorium for the last three days. The seizure was acute in onset, generalized tonic-clonic in nature, and associated with tongue biting, blood-tinged frothing, and urinary incontinence. It was her second episode of abnormal body movement and altered sensorium after being admitted to the hospital. Each episode lasted for 20-30 minutes. She returned from a hill station a few days ago, and her family and social history were insignificant.\nOn admission, the patient was afebrile, with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, a Spo2 of 97%, and a heart rate of 86/min. Her physical examination revealed a Glasgow coma scale (GCS) score of 11 (eye: 4, verbal: 2, motor: 5). Her rest systemic examination was unremarkable. Her initial laboratory investigations were within normal range except for mild elevation of transaminases (Table 1). Further evaluation with a non-contrast computed tomography (CT) head shows a ring-enhancing lesion in the left temporal region with perifocal edema, an eccentric, and an inflammatory granuloma (Figure 1). In addition, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) brain showed a well-defined rounded discrete hyperintense lesion in the left parietal region with mild to moderate vasogenic edema, leading to partial effacement of the adjoining sub-arachnoid space and moderate peripheral rim enhancement (Figure 2). On follow-up after two weeks, an MR venogram revealed that the left transverse sinus was hypoplastic, as shown in Figure 3.\nFollowing confirmation of diagnosis, the patient was started on albendazole 400mg twice daily and carbamazepine. However, on follow-up after two weeks, the patient developed skin rashes, remarkably elevated liver enzymes, and total bilirubin (Figure 4).\nAfter proper evaluation, immediately anti-epileptic drugs were discontinued, and for skin rashes, the patient was given oral fexofenadine and methylprednisolone, levetiracetam beside topical steroids. On subsequent follow-up, her rashes and deranged aminases resolved, and the patient was doing well.",
    "extracted_disease_name": "Neurocysticercosis",
    "grouped_disease_name": "Neurocysticercosis"
  }

    "content": "[
  {
    "case_text": "A 26-year-old Nepalese lady with no previous medical history presented with chief complaints of headache and left-sided monocular blurry vision for one week duration. The headache was located in the occipital region and was intermittent with no aggravating or relieving factors. There was no fever, nausea, vomiting or photophobia. She denied any sick contacts, animal exposure, history of recent infections, night sweats, weight loss, loss of appetite or change in bowel habits. Family history was negative, and her last international travel was to Nepal four months prior to presentation. She was married, had one child, and worked in catering department. Of note, there was no handling of pork meat as part of her job. Vital signs were normal and cranial nerve examination revealed left-sided ptosis and dilated left pupil with sluggish reaction to light. There was mild impairment in adduction, however other extraocular movements were preserved. There were no sensory or motor deficits in any of the extremities and meningeal signs were absent. Rest of the systemic examination was normal. Laboratory workup revealed normal complete blood count, renal and liver function tests. Her inflammatory markers including C-reactive protein and ESR were also normal. Computed tomography (CT) scan of the brain showed a focal hyperdense calcified lesion measuring 6 mm in the midbrain and a calcified focal lesion measuring 5 mm in the left frontal lobe. There was no midline shift or hydrocephalus, and the lesions were interpreted as calcified granulomas (<PMC10197005_gr1_undivided_1_1.webp>). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain showed a midbrain ring-enhancing lesion with central nodular calcification and significant peri-lesional edema. Another ring-enhancing nodular calcified lesion without edema was reported in the left parasagittal frontal lobe giving an overall impression of granulomatous disease consistent with NCC (<PMC10197005_gr2_undivided_1_1.webp>). Due to these findings, enzyme-linked immunoelectrotransfer blot (EITB) for anticysticercal antibodies was sent and was reportedly positive. Neurology and infectious disease teams were consulted, and the patient was started on a tapering regimen of dexamethasone for 14 days, in addition to albendazole and praziquantel. The patient was discharged four days later after improvement in ptosis and blurry vision. Unfortunately, post-discharge follow up was not established as the patient travelled back to her home country.",

    "case_text": "An 8-year-old female Ethiopian child presented to the hospital with a 4-month history of global headache, which worsened progressively. Otherwise, she had no abnormal body movements or gait disturbances.\nPhysical examination revealed intact cranial nerves and no sensory or motor deficits. Chest X-ray and abdominal ultrasound were normal. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain showed a cystic signal intensity similar to that of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), without ring enhancement. The axial T1-weighted brain MRI showed a well-defined hypointense lesion in the right temporal lobe with significant mass effect and midline shift (Figure 1). Coronal T2-weighted and axial fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) images of the brain showed a well-defined T2 hyperintense lesion that was totally suppressed on the FLAIR sequence, with no sign of perilesional edema (Figure 2).  \nThe sagittal and axial T1-weighted MRI of the brain after gadolinium administration showed a well-defined cystic mass with no enhancement on the cyst wall, and there was no mass-like or mural nodular enhancement (Figure 3). \nBased on the presentation and brain MRI findings, the diagnosis of giant intracranial hydatid cyst was made. Right temporoparietal craniotomy was carried out and a huge cyst, measuring 15x13x12 cm, was removed with an intact capsule, with maximum care taken to avoid rupture and spillage (Figure 4). \nThe removed cyst was sent for histopathological examination. The gross section showed a 7x6x4 cm whitish cystectomy specimen with a smooth outer surface. On cut section, it was found to be a unilocular cyst filled with clear fluid and with a rough inner surface. The wall thickness was 1-2 mm. Microscopically, the histological sections showed a laminated acellular cyst wall with a nucleated germinal layer. No protoscolices were seen (Figure 5). \nPostoperatively, the patient was stable.   {
    "case_text": "A 54-year-old Caucasian female presented to the neurosurgery service with a 1-day history of progressive right sided hemiparesis with 4/5 strength in both right upper and lower extremities. Past medical history was significant only for hypertension. The social history revealed that the patient resided on a farm during her childhood. There was no evidence of immunocompromise or systemic disease involving lung or other organs. Laboratory investigations were unremarkable. Initial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was inconclusive and follow-up imaging was performed at a 1-month interval after a dexamethasone wean with short-term clinical improvement in strength. At one month, and correlating with the steroid wean, there was progression in deficit with increasing weakness similar in degree to that found at presentation. Due to the uncertain diagnosis and worsening clinical symptoms, open surgical biopsy with possible resection was performed.\nMRI performed shortly after presentation [Figure 1] demonstrates a 2 x 4 cm heterogeneous intracranial mass lesion involving the left motor cortex and extending into the ipsilateral parietal lobe. The lesion was hypointense on T1 sequences and had a speckled appearance upon gadolinium administration, hyperintense on T2 sequences with minimal diffusion restriction on diffusion weighted imaging (DWI). Specifically, on T2 imaging there was lack of a defined focal cavity and absence of intracavitary projections with a crenated appearance. Single and multi-voxel MRS was also performed over the area of enhancement as well as the contralateral normal brain. Single-voxel MRS over the enhancing area demonstrated diminished N-acetyl aspartate (NAA) to creatine ratio (1.10), normal choline to NAA ratio (0.82), normal choline to creatine ratio (0.9), and a diminished myoinositol to creatine ratio (0.39). There appeared to be peaks between 3.6 and 3.8 ppm over the enhancing area that were not present in the contralateral normal brain [Figure 2]. Repeat MRI at one month demonstrated worsening edema consistent with worsening of the pathologic process [Figure 3].\nApproximately one month after presentation, the patient underwent a left fronto-parietal craniotomy for open surgical biopsy with possible resection of the lesion using frameless stereotactic guidance, intraoperative 3D-ultrasound, and neurophysiological monitoring with cortical/subcortical mapping. The neuropathologist's intraoperative impression of the frozen section was that of high-grade glioma. Therefore, resection was thus continued until subcortical stimulation of motor regions was achieved at three milliamps. Postoperatively no additional deficits were noted. The MRI performed on postoperative day one showed some residual enhancement along the splenium of the corpus callosum [<PMC4061578_SNI-5-80-g004_undivided_1_1.webp>]. The patient improved in strength by the time of discharge on postoperative day three. Following pathological confirmation, intravenous liposomal amphotericin B was initiated for 4 weeks, followed by oral itraconazole for 12 months. At last follow-up 1 year postoperation, cerebritis had resolved on MRI [<PMC4061578_SNI-5-80-g005_undivided_1_1.webp>] and her neurologic status was normal.\nHematoxylin and eosin staining showed chronic granulomatous inflammation with caseating necrosis, multinucleated giant cells, and epithelioid histiocytic cells [Figure 6] Periodic acid-Schiff staining showed broad based budding yeast, compatible with Blastomycosis [Figure 7]. full_prompt:
Case Text:
A 45-year-old Caucasian male patient presented at our emergency department with headache, visual deficiencies, and moderate disorientation. On the day of the admission, he experienced a convulsive seizure. His medical history was free of any severe infections, immunomodulatory diseases, severe traumas, malignancies, and recent surgical interventions. In the past years, he had not visited any tropical or subtropical areas.\nLaboratory parameters on admission were not deranged and physical examination showed normothermia without meningeal irritation. In addition to the visual deficiencies and the mild mnestic impairment, no neurological deficits could be identified.\nImmediate computed tomography (CT) scan showed a hypodense lesion in the right parietal region. On contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a ring-shaped lesion in the right parietal lobe was noted [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_a_1_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_b_2_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_c_3_4.webp>]

    "entity_type": "anatomical_location",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Brain**\n\nThe brain serves as the central nervous system (CNS) organ, housing multiple critical structures responsible for processing sensory input, regulating motor function, and maintaining homeostasis. While the brain is the primary site for most space-occupying lesions, its involvement is not universally common, particularly in certain clinical contexts. Lesions within the brain may manifest in MRI scans as focal or diffuse abnormalities, often characterized by alterations in tissue density, contrast enhancement, or signal intensity depending on etiology (e.g., neoplastic, inflammatory, or ischemic).\n\nIn cases of space-occupying lesions, the brain’s location within the cranial cavity dictates clinical presentation, including mass effect phenomena such as herniation syndromes, elevated intracranial pressure, and associated systemic sequelae. When sampled for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, the CNS region sampled may include cortical, subcortical, or deep gray matter structures, depending on the lesion’s laterality and depth.\n\nNotably, severe meningioencephalitis—an inflammatory process affecting both the meninges and brain parenchyma—can lead to fatal outcomes, such as the patient’s reported sudden cardiac arrest. This underscores the potential for life-threatening complications when CNS involvement is severe or untreated. While the brain itself is a fundamental anatomical entity, its clinical relevance extends to systemic interactions, particularly in conditions like meningioencephalitis, where multisystem involvement may occur.<SEP>brain",

    "description": "**Clinical Summary of Lesions**\n\nA *lesion* denotes an abnormal pathological finding that may manifest across various anatomical domains, including soft tissues, bony structures, and visceral organs. In the temporal bone, lesions are identified as structural abnormalities detectable via imaging, though their etiology—whether neoplastic, inflammatory, or degenerative—remains indeterminate. When intracranial, lesions frequently exceed 2 cm in diameter, with laterality variably documented, though bilateral involvement in the parietal lobes is noted in some cases.\n\nCardiovascular-related terminology, such as an *abnormality in the right coronary artery*, is included but does not constitute a traditional lesion. Chronicity is evident, as lesions persist for at least three months, raising concerns for underlying etiologies such as infection, ischemic changes, or neoplastic progression. Recurrence at surgical sites suggests a persistent pathological process, potentially indicating an aggressive or high-risk lesion type.\n\nMRI findings further detail lesions as hyperintense masses or consistent with progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) lesions. Specific localization includes the left parietal lobe, with generalized descriptions indicating bilateral involvement in the brain. Clinical ambiguity persists regarding lesion etiology, staging, or laterality in some cases, particularly when terms like *\"abnormal finding\"* are used without specific diagnostic criteria.   {
    "entity_name": "brain abscess",
    "entity_type": "disease_disorder",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Brain Abscess**\n\nA **brain abscess** represents a focal, localized infectious lesion within the brain parenchyma or adjacent structures, characterized by purulent material accumulation. Imaging studies, including computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), commonly demonstrate a ring-enhancing lesion, often in the right frontal lobe, with medial extension into the frontal horn of the right lateral ventricle. While the right frontal region is frequently affected, abscesses may also occur in other cerebral regions, including the temporo-parietal-occipital junction. The diagnosis is typically confirmed by the presence of thick purulent fluid within the lesion, often linked to systemic or local infectious sources such as dental or sinus infections.\n\nClinical presentations vary based on lesion size, location, and surrounding edema, often manifesting as focal neurological deficits, headache, or altered mental status. Differential diagnoses include metastatic tumors, granulomatous infections, and other ring-enhancing lesions, though the purulent nature and imaging characteristics strongly support the diagnosis of an abscess. Management generally involves a combination of surgical drainage (e.g., stereotactic aspiration or craniotomy) and systemic antimicrobial therapy, tailored to the identified pathogen. Comorbidities such as immunosuppression or dental infections may influence prognosis and treatment planning.\n\nNotably, the provided descriptions indicate variability in suspected locations, including the left cerebellar lobe, though the primary clinical consideration remains consistent with the general clinical presentation and diagnostic criteria outlined above. The presence of *Bacillus cereus* further supports the diagnosis in specific clinical contexts.<SEP>brain abscess described as locoregional extension from infection<SEP>patient's prior diagnosis of brain infection<SEP>brain abscess detected by MRI.",
    "source_id": "chunk-0472b50f5cb914128734e0366f5ba80c<SEP>chunk-343dd08aa0fb61dda463dd3232b2ea49<SEP>chunk-f1e51205d45bb6eaef20918c60002b6c<SEP>chunk-46b74499065467aff3cc9459270e8b51<SEP>chunk-42edd9d79524d48e1fc8f5917ed07134<SEP>chunk-140db4aa75c66dc6ba6b864e4ca81455<SEP>chunk-3941af76441132329cb7e2d64a4515ee<SEP>chunk-68b23069c2d996fb53f24de05aff6f03<SEP>chunk-128a7c45dbc5852ec655e5bd45a9118d<SEP>chunk-0239dc62bb59768c885404ab1ad7500e<SEP>chunk-d51497d4848f9dbbc6e26d5ddc994efb<SEP>chunk-5c7948c9a8c261d96d372edc4a8f57c1<SEP>chunk-49b66ff304496dd7262902f13707860f<SEP>chunk-df4ab0da38ee8c97952c224d0eb9fa62<SEP>chunk-f1c786ae357eeeda5dd64dafba515878<SEP>chunk-91e0a164234e11cf2484ab339fb1933d<SEP>chunk-2e9eb1615e454ad3cfa601e1bb410dd8<SEP>chunk-77206a7c1dc9f01c59b10be433055047<SEP>chunk-431f415d19a5db18dd338a2ec331340c<SEP>chunk-1a2522f8d2fe33172d656ffe363d5ea4",
    "file_path": "custom_case_12993.json<SEP>custom_case_15343.json<SEP>custom_case_17217.json<SEP>custom_case_22770.json<SEP>custom_case_6563.json<SEP>custom_case_2416.json<SEP>custom_case_6088.json<SEP>custom_case_10477.json<SEP>custom_case_13324.json<SEP>custom_case_15819.json<SEP>custom_case_177.json<SEP>custom_case_18359.json<SEP>custom_case_20922.json<SEP>custom_case_224.json<SEP>custom_case_4401.json<SEP>custom_case_15344.json<SEP>custom_case_20132.json<SEP>custom_case_828.json",

  {
    "entity_name": "edema",
    "entity_type": "clinical_event",
    "description": "**Edema** is a clinically significant condition characterized by the abnormal accumulation of interstitial fluid due to disturbances in capillary permeability, lymphatic function, or systemic hemodynamic balance. The provided descriptions highlight distinct presentations—ranging from localized inflammatory or infectious etiologies to systemic and neurological manifestations—requiring careful differentiation based on anatomical context, severity, and associated comorbidities.\n\n**Neurological Edema**: Cerebral edema, including findings in the cerebral cortex, temporal, and occipital lobes, often reflects intracranial pathology. Multifocal white matter changes (e.g., demyelinating plaques or toxic/metabolic encephalopathy) suggest underlying demyelinating diseases (e.g., multiple sclerosis) or encephalopathies. Urgent neuroimaging and metabolic screening are essential in suspected cases, given the risk of intracranial hypertension or herniation.\n\n**Spinal Cord Edema**: Histopathological evidence of spinal cord edema may indicate compressive or inflammatory processes, such as transverse myelitis, spinal cord tumors, or traumatic injury. Differential diagnosis includes vascular events (e.g., spinal artery occlusion) or infectious/inflammatory conditions (e.g., discitis).\n\n**Localized Edema**: Observed swelling in the left hand, cervicocranial region, or foot (left foot) often points to inflammatory/infectious causes (e.g., cellulitis, lymphadenitis) or post-surgical sequelae. Absence of edema on physical exam in some cases may mask occult systemic conditions (e.g., hypoalbuminemia, lymphatic obstruction). Transient soft tissue edema post-surgery may exacerbate in patients with diabetes mellitus or venous insufficiency.\n\n**Systemic Edema**: Bilateral lower limb edema, when present, aligns with systemic conditions such as congestive heart failure or nephrotic syndrome. The presence of gangrene and hypotension suggests severe systemic illness, necessitating hemodynamic and infectious disease evaluation. Systemic edema may also arise from hypoalbuminemia or lymphatic dysfunction, requiring renal and hepatic function assessment.\n\n**Clinical Implications**: Edema’s presentation varies widely, from localized inflammation to generalized fluid overload. Differential diagnosis must account for anatomical context, severity, and comorbidities. Neurological edema demands urgent neuroimaging and metabolic workup, while localized edema requires targeted infectious/inflammatory evaluation. Systemic edema necessitates hemodynamic and renal assessment, particularly in patients with hypotension or gangrene.     "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 45-year-old Caucasian male patient presented at our emergency department with headache, visual deficiencies, and moderate disorientation. On the day of the admission, he experienced a convulsive seizure. His medical history was free of any severe infections, immunomodulatory diseases, severe traumas, malignancies, and recent surgical interventions. In the past years, he had not visited any tropical or subtropical areas.\\nLaboratory parameters on admission were not deranged and physical examination showed normothermia without meningeal irritation. In addition to the visual deficiencies and the mild mnestic impairment, no neurological deficits could be identified.\\nImmediate computed tomography (CT) scan showed a hypodense lesion in the right parietal region. On contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a ring-shaped lesion in the right parietal lobe was noted [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_a_1_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_b_2_4.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_c_3_4.webp>]. The diffusion-weighted images showed an intensive hyperintense signal, which was strongly suspicious for a brain abscess [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g001_d_4_4.webp>].\\nCalculated antibiotic treatment was initiated with amoxicillin/clavulanic acid and metronidazole. The patient was prepared for surgical intervention. The patient was informed regarding the off-label use of FL, and he provided written consent.\\nApproximately 30-45 minutes prior to skin incision, 5 mg/kg bodyweight of FL (Natrium Fluoreszein  10%, ALCON, Germany) was administered via the central venous line during induction of anesthesia.\\nCraniotomy was planned with neuronavigation. After durotomy, the PENTERO 900 surgical microscope was applied. The lesion could not be detected under white light, however, showed intensive fluorescent staining under the YE560 nm filter [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g002_a_1_2.webp><PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g002_b_2_2.webp>]. After opening of the capsule, viscous pus poured out [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g003_a_1_2.webp>] that was not fluorescing under filtered light [<PMC5180436_SNI-7-955-g003_b_2_2.webp>]. The pus and the capsule were microsurgically removed and sent to the neuropathology and microbiology department.\\nThe postoperative course was uneventful. Because no microbiological specimen could be identified, calculated antibiotic treatment was continued. The visual deficiencies and the mnestic impairment completely resolved, and the patient was discharged after 5 weeks.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Brain abscess\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Abscess\"\n  }\n]\n\nAttached image descriptions:\n- CT scan of the head.\nMass in the right frontal lobe.\n- T1-weighted MRI of the brain.\nLesion in the right parietal lobe.\nHypointense area.\nNo other visible abnormalities.\n- CT scan of the brain.\nMass in the right frontal lobe.\n- Brain MRI.\nLesion in the right parietal lobe.\nLocation: right parietal lobe.\n- CT scan of the chest.\nNodule in the right lung.\nSize and location of the nodule are not specified.",

    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 24-year-old female was brought to the emergency department with complaints of altered mental status and recurrent seizures associated with low-grade fever, headache, and altered sensorium for the last three days. The seizure was acute in onset, generalized tonic-clonic in nature, and associated with tongue biting, blood-tinged frothing, and urinary incontinence. It was her second episode of abnormal body movement and altered sensorium after being admitted to the hospital. Each episode lasted for 20-30 minutes. She returned from a hill station a few days ago, and her family and social history were insignificant.\\nOn admission, the patient was afebrile, with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, a Spo2 of 97%, and a heart rate of 86/min. Her physical examination revealed a Glasgow coma scale (GCS) score of 11 (eye: 4, verbal: 2, motor: 5). Her rest systemic examination was unremarkable. Her initial laboratory investigations were within normal range except for mild elevation of transaminases (Table 1). Further evaluation with a non-contrast computed tomography (CT) head shows a ring-enhancing lesion in the left temporal region with perifocal edema, an eccentric, and an inflammatory granuloma (Figure 1). In addition, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) brain showed a well-defined rounded discrete hyperintense lesion in the left parietal region with mild to moderate vasogenic edema, leading to partial effacement of the adjoining sub-arachnoid space and moderate peripheral rim enhancement (Figure 2). On follow-up after two weeks, an MR venogram revealed that the left transverse sinus was hypoplastic, as shown in Figure 3.\\nFollowing confirmation of diagnosis, the patient was started on albendazole 400mg twice daily and carbamazepine. However, on follow-up after two weeks, the patient developed skin rashes, remarkably elevated liver enzymes, and total bilirubin (Figure 4).\\nAfter proper evaluation, immediately anti-epileptic drugs were discontinued, and for skin rashes, the patient was given oral fexofenadine and methylprednisolone, levetiracetam beside topical steroids. On subsequent follow-up, her rashes and deranged aminases resolved, and the patient was doing well.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Neurocysticercosis\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Neurocysticercosis\"\n  }\n]",

    "reference_id": "4",
    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 26-year-old Nepalese lady with no previous medical history presented with chief complaints of headache and left-sided monocular blurry vision for one week duration. The headache was located in the occipital region and was intermittent with no aggravating or relieving factors. There was no fever, nausea, vomiting or photophobia. She denied any sick contacts, animal exposure, history of recent infections, night sweats, weight loss, loss of appetite or change in bowel habits. Family history was negative, and her last international travel was to Nepal four months prior to presentation. She was married, had one child, and worked in catering department. Of note, there was no handling of pork meat as part of her job. Vital signs were normal and cranial nerve examination revealed left-sided ptosis and dilated left pupil with sluggish reaction to light. There was mild impairment in adduction, however other extraocular movements were preserved. There were no sensory or motor deficits in any of the extremities and meningeal signs were absent. Rest of the systemic examination was normal. Laboratory workup revealed normal complete blood count, renal and liver function tests. Her inflammatory markers including C-reactive protein and ESR were also normal. Computed tomography (CT) scan of the brain showed a focal hyperdense calcified lesion measuring 6 mm in the midbrain and a calcified focal lesion measuring 5 mm in the left frontal lobe. There was no midline shift or hydrocephalus, and the lesions were interpreted as calcified granulomas (<PMC10197005_gr1_undivided_1_1.webp>). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain showed a midbrain ring-enhancing lesion with central nodular calcification and significant peri-lesional edema. Another ring-enhancing nodular calcified lesion without edema was reported in the left parasagittal frontal lobe giving an overall impression of granulomatous disease consistent with NCC (<PMC10197005_gr2_undivided_1_1.webp>). Due to these findings, enzyme-linked immunoelectrotransfer blot (EITB) for anticysticercal antibodies was sent and was reportedly positive. Neurology and infectious disease teams were consulted, and the patient was started on a tapering regimen of dexamethasone for 14 days, in addition to albendazole and praziquantel. The patient was discharged four days later after improvement in ptosis and blurry vision. Unfortunately, post-discharge follow up was not established as the patient travelled back to her home country.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Neurocysticercosis\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Neurocysticercosis\"\n  }\n]\n\nAttached image descriptions:\n- CT head shows a mass in the right frontal lobe.\n- *   Brain image\n*   Lesion in the right parietal lobe",

    "reference_id": "5",
    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"An 8-year-old female Ethiopian child presented to the hospital with a 4-month history of global headache, which worsened progressively. She also had projectile non-bilious vomiting two or three times a day and blurring of her vision for a duration of 1 month. Otherwise, she had no abnormal body movements or gait disturbances.\\nPhysical examination revealed intact cranial nerves and no sensory or motor deficits. Chest X-ray and abdominal ultrasound were normal. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain showed a cystic signal intensity similar to that of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), without ring enhancement. The axial T1-weighted brain MRI showed a well-defined hypointense lesion in the right temporal lobe with significant mass effect and midline shift (Figure 1). Coronal T2-weighted and axial fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) images of the brain showed a well-defined T2 hyperintense lesion that was totally suppressed on the FLAIR sequence, with no sign of perilesional edema (Figure 2).  \\nThe sagittal and axial T1-weighted MRI of the brain after gadolinium administration showed a well-defined cystic mass with no enhancement on the cyst wall, and there was no mass-like or mural nodular enhancement (Figure 3). \\nBased on the presentation and brain MRI findings, the diagnosis of giant intracranial hydatid cyst was made. Right temporoparietal craniotomy was carried out and a huge cyst, measuring 15x13x12 cm, was removed with an intact capsule, with maximum care taken to avoid rupture and spillage (Figure 4). \\nThe removed cyst was sent for histopathological examination. The gross section showed a 7x6x4 cm whitish cystectomy specimen with a smooth outer surface. On cut section, it was found to be a unilocular cyst filled with clear fluid and with a rough inner surface. The wall thickness was 1-2 mm. Microscopically, the histological sections showed a laminated acellular cyst wall with a nucleated germinal layer. No protoscolices were seen (Figure 5). \\nPostoperatively, the patient was stable. Albendazole 15 mg/kg was initiated and continued for 4 weeks. The patient showed marked improvement with resolution of her symptoms.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Cerebral hydatid cyst\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Echinococcosis\"\n  }\n]",

    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"Case 2: 49-Year-old man with HIV presented with left-sided weakness and seizures. Initial HF-MRI T2-FLAIR showed extensive white matter lesions in the right hemisphere, suggesting PML. A confluent PML lesion had contrast enhancement on post-GBCA T1WI and slight edema, suggesting active inflammation. Same-day pULF-MRI recapitulated these lesions on T2-FLAIR and showed contrast-enhancing areas on T1WI.     "reference_id": "7",
    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 52-year-old male was admitted with a paroxysmal headache in the right parietal region accompanied by visual disturbance in the right eye for over 2 months. Both symptoms mostly occurred in the morning and could be partially relieved with ibuprofen. He had a 2-year history of diabetes mellitus type 2 well controlled with metformin and repaglinide. He was afebrile and neurological examination was unremarkable. CT scan showed a mass in sphenoid sinus and cavernous sinus with bone destruction (Fig. 2a). MRI showed a 20 x 25 mm circular intrasellar mass with hyperintensity T1WI and T2WI, heterogeneously gadolinium-enhancement, and adjacent meningeal enhancement. The lesion extended bilaterally into the cavernous sinus and formed a 12 x 11 mm mass (Fig. 2b-d). The patient underwent a trans-sphenoidal mass excision and debridement. The lesion was solid and cystic with yellow-brown fluid and a gray wax-like solid component. A biopsy was performed immediately and histopathologic examination revealed Aspergillus species (Fig. 2e). Post-operative physical and neurological examination showed obvious improvement of headache and visual disturbance. Post-operative images indicated the resection of the mass (Fig. 2f). Examination after a follow-up of 20 months showed no clinical symptoms.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Aspergillosis\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Fungal Infection\"\n  }\n]",

    "reference_id": "8",
    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 37-year-old, ambidextrous taxidermist presented to ophthalmology clinic at the University of Alabama at Birmingham Hospital with a several year history of visual floaters of the right eye and intermittent decreased hearing on the left. He complained of worsening symptoms over the previous 8 months with intermittent sensations of pressure behind the right eye, difficulty with balance and ambulation, difficulty reading, and mild headaches. He also described poorly defined problems with cognition. He did not report the impairment of speech, language, or comprehension. His past medical and surgical histories were unremarkable. He denied the use of tobacco or intravenous drugs and indicated infrequent alcohol use.\\nOn physical examination, significant findings included a small right inferior quadrantanopia to confrontational examination. Automated visual fields documented the presence of a congruous inferior quadrantanopia. His neurologic examination was otherwise normal. No abnormal skin findings were present.\\nAn magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain was obtained and revealed two closely associated large, heterogeneous, left temporo-occipital lesions with mass effect. The lesions were heterogeneously enhancing on T1-weighted images, and T2-weighted images showed changes consistent with edema extending through the temporal and occipital lobes. Diffusion-weighted imaging demonstrated equivocal increased signal intensity within the lesions. A low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) was not seen on the ADC map, as is typical with a cerebral abscesses. While closely associated with the convexity dura and the tentorium, the masses appeared to be intra-axial, and were interpreted as most consistent with malignant glioma or metastatic lesion [<PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g001_a_1_3.webp><PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g001_b_2_3.webp><PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g001_c_3_3.webp>]. Of note, his blood profile indicated mild leukocytosis (12 x 103/mm3) and predominantly neutrophilic (74%). Lumbar puncture was deferred because of intracranial mass effect. Body imaging did not reveal extracranial lesions and urine cultures were negative.\\nFor diagnosis and therapy, the patient was taken to the operating room for a neuronavigation-guided resection of the mass via a temporo-occipital craniotomy with ultrasound assistance in delineating the borders of the lesion. The intra-operative specimen appeared to be a fungal abscess on frozen section evaluation. The abscess was resected [<PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g002_undivided_1_1.webp>] and the patient was started on intravenous lipid complex amphotericin B and flucytosine, which he received for approximately 3 weeks. The microbiology cultures demonstrated B. dermatitidis. The amphotericin B and flucytosine were stopped and the patient was electively prescribed oral voriconazole 200 mg twice a day.\\nHistopathology revealed a granulomatous process with encapsulated yeast forms. Numerous multinucleated giant cells were present, some of which contained fungal organisms, which were small, refractile, and variably-sized. A CD163 stain demonstrated abundant macrophages, and also highlighted the larger, multinucleated histiocytic giant cells [<PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g003_A_1_1.webp>]. The fungal organisms were positive for both periodic acid-Schiff and Gomori's methenamine silver [<PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g004_a_1_2.webp><PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g004_b_2_2.webp>] stains. There was an abundance of T cells, with a slight predominance of the CD4 helper/inducer subset as compared to the CD8 cytotoxic/suppressor subset [Figure 5]. Natural killer cells were rarely present [Figure 6]. The abscess displayed a sharp border with the underlying gliotic brain tissue.\\nThe patient did well with voriconazole therapy and was followed up for possible voriconazole toxicity with liver function tests, which continued to be stable at 8 months past the initiation of therapy. Due to the development of photosensitivity and some areas of cutaneous erythema, voriconazole was stopped after 7 months of therapy and switched to oral fluconazole 800 mg daily. This was continued for 3 months. At 12 months postoperatively, the patient was doing well and showed gradual improvement in his visual field deficit. His MRI at this time showed small lobulated areas of enhancement that were interpreted as postsurgical change and not a recurrence of the abscess [<PMC4665129_SNI-6-174-g007_undivided_1_1.webp>]. All antifungal therapy was stopped. The patient has continued to do well at 21/2 years postoperatively with a stable right inferior quadrantanopia on ophthalmologic examination.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Blastomycosis\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Fungal Infection\"\n  }\n]\n\nAttached image descriptions:\n- Brain CT scan.\nLesion in the right parietal lobe.\nLocation: right parietal lobe.\n- The image is a CT     "reference_id": "9",
    "content": "[\n  {\n    \"case_text\": \"A 54-year-old Caucasian female presented to the neurosurgery service with a 1-day history of progressive right sided hemiparesis with 4/5 strength in both right upper and lower extremities. Past medical history was significant only for hypertension. The social history revealed that the patient resided on a farm during her childhood. There was no evidence of immunocompromise or systemic disease involving lung or other organs. Laboratory investigations were unremarkable. Initial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was inconclusive and follow-up imaging was performed at a 1-month interval after a dexamethasone wean with short-term clinical improvement in strength. At one month, and correlating with the steroid wean, there was progression in deficit with increasing weakness similar in degree to that found at presentation. Due to the uncertain diagnosis and worsening clinical symptoms, open surgical biopsy with possible resection was performed.\\nMRI performed shortly after presentation [Figure 1] demonstrates a 2 x 4 cm heterogeneous intracranial mass lesion involving the left motor cortex and extending into the ipsilateral parietal lobe. The lesion was hypointense on T1 sequences and had a speckled appearance upon gadolinium administration, hyperintense on T2 sequences with minimal diffusion restriction on diffusion weighted imaging (DWI). Specifically, on T2 imaging there was lack of a defined focal cavity and absence of intracavitary projections with a crenated appearance. Single and multi-voxel MRS was also performed over the area of enhancement as well as the contralateral normal brain. Single-voxel MRS over the enhancing area demonstrated diminished N-acetyl aspartate (NAA) to creatine ratio (1.10), normal choline to NAA ratio (0.82), normal choline to creatine ratio (0.9), and a diminished myoinositol to creatine ratio (0.39). There appeared to be peaks between 3.6 and 3.8 ppm over the enhancing area that were not present in the contralateral normal brain [Figure 2]. Repeat MRI at one month demonstrated worsening edema consistent with worsening of the pathologic process [Figure 3].\\nApproximately one month after presentation, the patient underwent a left fronto-parietal craniotomy for open surgical biopsy with possible resection of the lesion using frameless stereotactic guidance, intraoperative 3D-ultrasound, and neurophysiological monitoring with cortical/subcortical mapping. The neuropathologist's intraoperative impression of the frozen section was that of high-grade glioma. Therefore, resection was thus continued until subcortical stimulation of motor regions was achieved at three milliamps. Postoperatively no additional deficits were noted. The MRI performed on postoperative day one showed some residual enhancement along the splenium of the corpus callosum [<PMC4061578_SNI-5-80-g004_undivided_1_1.webp>]. The patient improved in strength by the time of discharge on postoperative day three. Following pathological confirmation, intravenous liposomal amphotericin B was initiated for 4 weeks, followed by oral itraconazole for 12 months. At last follow-up 1 year postoperation, cerebritis had resolved on MRI [<PMC4061578_SNI-5-80-g005_undivided_1_1.webp>] and her neurologic status was normal.\\nHematoxylin and eosin staining showed chronic granulomatous inflammation with caseating necrosis, multinucleated giant cells, and epithelioid histiocytic cells [Figure 6] Periodic acid-Schiff staining showed broad based budding yeast, compatible with Blastomycosis [Figure 7]. The specimen was sent to the Centers for Disease Control and they confirmed the diagnosis of Blastomycosis.\",\n    \"extracted_disease_name\": \"Blastomycosis\",\n    \"grouped_disease_name\": \"Fungal Infection\"\n  }\n]\n\nAttached image descriptions:\n- CT scan of the brain.\nLesion in the right parietal lobe.\n- T2-weighted MRI of the brain.\nLesion in the left parietal lobe.\n- *   CT scan of the chest\n*   Nodule in the right lung\n- CT scan of the chest.\nNodule in the right lung.",

case_text: A 13-months old Sudanese male presented with low grade fever and irritability. He was diagnosed with acute otitis media and placed on oral amoxicillin. Fever and irritability initially resolved but on day 7 of amoxicillin, fever (102 F) returned along with leukocytosis (20,000 cells/muL). Intramuscular ceftriaxone was given but within 24 h, he had developed vomiting, right arm weakness, worsening irritability, and persistent fever. Because of the onset of neurologic symptoms, a CT scan of the brain was performed and showed a large left parietal brain mass with midline shift and cerebral edema (<PMC7188777_fped-08-00117-g0001_undivided_1_1.webp>).

    "entity_type": "clinical_event",
    "description": "**Respiratory Failure**\n\nRespiratory failure, defined as the inability to sustain adequate gas exchange, represents a critical clinical syndrome characterized by hypoxemia and/or hypercapnia, arising from diverse pathophysiological mechanisms. The descriptions highlight its prominence in both acute and chronic settings, particularly in patients with underlying cardiopulmonary disease, where conditions such as **pulmonary edema** frequently precipitate rapid deterioration through alveolar ventilation-perfusion mismatch. In cardiogenic contexts, fluid overload disrupts gas exchange, leading to hypoxemic respiratory failure.\n\nA significant subset of cases involves **Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS)**, where autoimmune-mediated peripheral demyelination disrupts neuromuscular transmission, predisposing up to 70% of patients to respiratory muscle paralysis within days to weeks of symptom onset. This progression often culminates in ventilatory failure if untreated, contributing substantially to morbidity and mortality within the first month of hospitalization.\n\nAcute respiratory failure also emerges as a cause of death in febrile illnesses, where infectious or inflammatory processes may exacerbate respiratory compromise through direct lung injury (e.g., pneumonia) or systemic effects (e.g., sepsis-induced **acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)**). The descriptions suggest predominant forms of hypoxemic respiratory failure, with systemic involvement affecting both central and peripheral respiratory drive mechanisms.\n\nClinical presentations are marked by variable severity, though laterality or specific anatomical involvement is not detailed. No explicit staging criteria (e.g., Type I/II respiratory failure) or dosing guidelines for interventions are provided, though supportive therapies—such as oxygen therapy, ventilatory support, or non-invasive ventilation—are routinely employed in severe cases. Underlying comorbidities, including cardiovascular disease or neuromuscular disorders, are implied but not explicitly described.     "entity_type": "sign_symptom",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Seizures**\n\nSeizures constitute a heterogeneous neurological phenomenon characterized by transient, excessive neuronal discharges, manifesting as motor, sensory, autonomic, or cognitive disturbances. The provided descriptions highlight key clinical variability, including both symptomatic and asymptomatic presentations, as well as distinct associations with underlying conditions.\n\nIn several instances, seizures are documented as a presenting symptom, often reflecting underlying neurological, metabolic, or structural pathology. Notably, seizures may complicate acute neurological emergencies, such as intracranial hemorrhage or subarachnoid hemorrhage, particularly when associated with sudden onset of severe headache followed by continuous epileptic activity. The progression to increased seizure frequency suggests worsening encephalopathy, structural brain lesions, or evolving epilepsy.\n\nPharmacogenic risks further complicate seizure management. *Mefloquine* is contraindicated in patients with a history of seizures due to its strict contraindication, likely stemming from CNS toxicity or drug interactions. Similarly, *metronidazole* (e.g., *Flagyl*) has been associated with severe adverse effects, including recurrent seizures, necessitating careful monitoring in high-risk populations.\n\nClinical heterogeneity is evident in cases of sudden onset in previously seizure-free patients, underscoring the importance of diagnostic suspicion. Conversely, the absence of prior seizure history may indicate preexisting control or asymptomatic status, though longitudinal monitoring remains essential to detect recurrence or decompensation.\n\nDistinct clinical entities are identified, including:\n- **Seizures in pediatric patients**, documented in one child.\n- **Seizures as a contraindication for mefloquine**, reflecting documented risk in specific populations.\n- **Seizures as a presenting symptom** in cases of acute neurological emergencies or progressive neurological disease.\n\nOverall, seizures represent a multifaceted clinical entity requiring individualized assessment, including etiology, severity, and associated comorbidities. Further differentiation is warranted to distinguish idiopathic, symptomatic, or provoked seizures, with particular attention to pharmacogenic risks and acute precipitants.",

    "entity_name": "Cryptococcus neoformans",
    "entity_type": "organism",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Cryptococcus neoformans Infection**\n\n*Cryptococcus neoformans* is a dimorphic, encapsulated yeast pathogen primarily known for its role in systemic infections, particularly *cryptococcosis*, which may manifest across multiple organ systems. The organism’s capsular polysaccharide composition—comprising glucuronoxylomannan (GXM)—plays a critical role in immune evasion and diagnostic detection via serological assays, such as cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) agglutination titers. Clinical presentations are highly variable, with *biliary cryptococcosis* emerging as a distinct entity when inferred from elevated CrAg titers in biliary specimens, though direct isolation from bile remains rare. Systemic dissemination often involves hematogenous spread, evidenced by positive blood cultures in advanced disease stages.\n\nCentral nervous system (CNS) involvement, particularly *cryptococcal meningitis*, is the most common clinical manifestation, characterized by yeast organisms identified in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis. Elevated CrAg titers (e.g., 1:2 in CSF, 1:64 in serum) correlate with disease burden and are used for both diagnosis and monitoring. While *C. neoformans* is classically associated with pulmonary and CNS infections, its isolation from blood cultures suggests disseminated disease, often in immunocompromised hosts (e.g., individuals with HIV/AIDS, solid organ transplant recipients, or those on immunosuppressive therapy). The pathogen’s polysaccharide composition facilitates immune evasion, contributing to its ability to persist despite host defenses.<SEP>medium quantity of Cryptococcus neoformans in the CSF<SEP>mNGS analysis of the puncture specimen revealed the presence of Cryptococcus neoformans<SEP>multiple variable-sized encapsulated yeast cells present in hematoxylin-eosin, hexamine silver, and periodic acid-Schiff staining",

    "entity_name": "lumbar puncture",
    "entity_type": "therapeutic_procedure",
    "description": "**Lumbar Puncture (LP)**\n\nThe lumbar puncture, or spinal tap, is a diagnostic and therapeutic procedure performed to aspirate or inject cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) via the subarachnoid space at the lumbar region, most commonly between the L3–L4 or L4–L5 intervertebral spaces. This technique is employed to evaluate conditions such as meningitis (e.g., bacterial, viral, or fungal), multiple sclerosis, neoplastic processes (e.g., primary CNS lymphoma, metastatic disease), or intracranial pressure disorders (e.g., idiopathic intracranial hypertension). Diagnostic LP may include CSF collection for laboratory analysis, including viral panels, cytology, and microbiology studies to identify infectious agents, neoplastic cells, or inflammatory mediators.\n\nIn select cases, abnormal CSF findings have been documented, such as a leukocyte count of **20 WBC/μL** (2,030 RBC/μL) with **33% neutrophils**, suggestive of an inflammatory or infectious etiology. Routine LPs, however, yielded normal results, including negative viral panels. Therapeutically, lumbar cistern drainage may be utilized in conditions of elevated intracranial pressure or CSF overproduction, such as in hydrocephalus or idiopathic intracranial hypertension.\n\nComplications of lumbar puncture are rare but may include postprocedural headache, infection, or dural puncture sequelae (e.g., transient neurological deficits). Standardized LPs performed on hospital days 4 and 5 were unremarkable. Additional diagnostic procedures included JCV-DNA testing with high sensitivity, with reported leukocyte count of **0.02/mm³**, albumin level of **0.85 g/L**, and glucose level of **0.61 g/L**. A negative Venereal Disease Research Laboratory (VDRL) test was noted, with marginally elevated protein at **0.43 g/L**.\n\nThe procedure was performed in at least one instance shortly after birth (within one week of delivery) and was attempted in other documented cases. Patient refusal before discharge precluded further evaluation in one instance.",

  {
    "entity_name": "brain abscess",
    "entity_type": "disease_disorder",
    "description": "**Clinical Summary: Brain Abscess**\n\nA **brain abscess** represents a focal, localized infectious lesion within the brain parenchyma or adjacent structures, characterized by purulent material accumulation. Imaging studies, including computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), commonly demonstrate a ring-enhancing lesion, often in the right frontal lobe, with medial extension into the frontal horn of the right lateral ventricle. While the right frontal region is frequently affected, abscesses may also occur in other cerebral regions, including the temporo-parietal-occipital junction. The diagnosis is typically confirmed by the presence of thick purulent fluid within the lesion, often linked to systemic or local infectious sources such as dental or sinus infections.\n\nClinical presentations vary based on lesion size, location, and surrounding edema, often manifesting as focal neurological deficits, headache, or altered mental status. Differential diagnoses include metastatic tumors, granulomatous infections, and other ring-enhancing lesions, though the purulent nature and imaging characteristics strongly support the diagnosis of an abscess. Management generally involves a combination of surgical drainage (e.g., stereotactic aspiration or craniotomy) and systemic antimicrobial therapy, tailored to the identified pathogen. Comorbidities such as immunosuppression or dental infections may influence prognosis and treatment planning.\n\nNotably, the provided descriptions indicate variability in suspected locations, including the left cerebellar lobe, though the primary clinical consideration remains consistent with the general clinical presentation and diagnostic criteria outlined above. The presence of *Bacillus cereus* further supports the diagnosis in specific clinical contexts.<SEP>brain abscess described as locoregional extension from infection<SEP>patient's prior diagnosis of brain infection<SEP>brain abscess detected by MRI.",

  {
    "entity_name": "scrub typhus",
    "entity_type": "disease_disorder",
    "description": "**Scrub Typhus: Clinical Overview**\n\nScrub typhus, caused by the obligate intracellular bacterium *Orientia tsutsugamushi*, is an arthropod-borne systemic illness transmitted primarily through the bite of *Leptotrombidium scutellare* (chigger) vectors. Endemic in tropical and subtropical regions of Asia, Oceania, and parts of Africa, it arises from dense vegetation supporting vector proliferation. The disease manifests in two distinct phases: an initial prodromal period characterized by high-grade fever, headache, and myalgias, followed by the development of a necrotic eschar at the bite site and regional lymphadenopathy.\n\nDiagnosis relies on serological testing, including detection of immunoglobulin M via indirect immunofluorescence assay (IFA) or enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). Indirect complement fixation (ICT) may also be employed, though negative results do not exclude infection if clinical suspicion remains high. Confirmatory diagnosis often combines positive serological findings (e.g., ICT and ELISA positivity) with clinical features. Differential considerations include other rickettsial infections (*Rickettsia prowazekii*, *Rickettsia akari*) and febrile illnesses such as dengue or malaria, necessitating clinical correlation and serological cross-reactivity awareness.\n\nComplications may include pneumonia, encephalitis, or multiorgan failure, particularly in untreated or severe cases. Early antibiotic treatment with doxycycline or chloramphenicol improves prognosis, though delayed therapy may result in severe sequelae. In endemic settings, maternal infection carries risks for vertical transmission, though neonatal scrub typhus remains rare; postnatal seroconversion or maternal infection confirmation typically supports neonatal diagnosis.",

  {
    "entity_name": "hydrocephalus",
    "entity_type": "clinical_event",
    "description": "**Hydrocephalus: A Comprehensive Clinical Summary**\n\nHydrocephalus is defined by an abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) within the brain’s ventricular system, resulting in ventricular dilation and elevated intracranial pressure. The clinical presentations vary based on etiology, laterality, and associated comorbidities, necessitating individualized diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.\n\nKey diagnostic features include radiographic confirmation via computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), revealing dilated ventricles. Common clinical manifestations include headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and signs of increased intracranial pressure. The condition may arise from congenital malformations, infectious or inflammatory processes (e.g., *coccidioidomycosis*-associated meningitis), or post-surgical changes, such as cerebellar peduncle atrophy or fourth ventricular lesions.\n\nIn pediatric cases, hydrocephalus frequently requires intervention, with ventriculo-peritoneal shunting (VPS) being the standard treatment for obstructive forms. Surgical history is frequently documented, particularly in patients with congenital or post-traumatic etiologies. Notably, hydrocephalus may complicate well-defined cystic lesions within the fourth ventricle, further complicating management. While radiographic worsening of hydrocephalus on MRI is a critical finding, the precise etiology—whether infectious, traumatic, or congenital—remains variably documented across cases. The presence of hydrocephalus at patient admission suggests a systemic or acute clinical context, often necessitating prompt evaluation and intervention.<SEP>mild hydrocephalus on brain ultrasound.<SEP>misdiagnosed diseases in neurosyphilis patients<SEP>without evidence for hydrocephalus<SEP>Hydrocephalus indicated by placement of left extraventricular drain.<SEP>Upstream hydrocephalus<SEP>Observed in brain tomography.",

  {
    "entity_name": "Cryptococcal meningitis",
    "entity_type": "disease_disorder",
    "description": "**Cryptococcal meningitis** represents an invasive fungal infection of the central nervous system (CNS) caused by *Cryptococcus neoformans*, characterized by meningoencephalitis with associated cryptococcal meningitis. The diagnosis is typically confirmed through lumbar puncture (LP) findings, including elevated opening pressure, cryptococcal antigen detection in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), and culture confirmation of *C. neoformans* (though negative culture results do not exclude the diagnosis, as suggested in the provided descriptions). Clinical suspicion arises in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with HIV/AIDS, solid-organ transplant recipients, or other conditions predisposing to disseminated cryptococcosis. The absence of bacterial, fungal, or acid-fast bacillus organisms on LP does not definitively exclude cryptococcal meningitis, necessitating additional diagnostic testing, including cryptococcal antigen serology (e.g., latex agglutination or enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay) and CSF cryptococcal antigen titers. The condition is often associated with systemic cryptococcosis, with CNS involvement occurring in up to 90% of disseminated cases.<SEP>Infection by *C. neoformans* causing meningitis<SEP>diagnosis based on CSF findings<SEP>diagnosis of CM made after finding Cryptococcus in the CSF",
